text_clean: So many have written about Machu Picchu one of the wonders of the world that I need not repeat their comments. Cutting to the chase, here are my recommendations: 1. Visit the Larco museum in Lima to see artifacts and learn about the Incas and Peruvian history and culture. Go late in the afternoon and dine at the beautiful and well rated museum restaurant. There is no museum at Aguas Caliente or Machu Picchu. The tour guides all have the same picture play book and stay at a superrficial level. 2. January was a good time to come despite possible bad weather. Upwards of 6,000 people a day come here so plan ahead and come as early as possible. 3. A bus takes you to the top and outstanding views are to be had. Sit on the driver's side going up and the non-driver side coming down. Better yet hike up! 6 hours but worth it. Or you can ride to the top on horseback. 4. The weather, and therefore the views, are changeable. A 1,000 inches of rain come here annually. Be prepared for ad weather. 5. Although the ruins are interesting, our group agreed that the views are what take your breath away. 6. Consider taking high altitude medicine, prescription required. Note: no restrooms after the entrance gate. 7. In Aguas Caliente, consider booking a massage afterwards. 8. Do not do a one day trip from Cusco, there and back. Arrive at Aguas Caliente the day before and hit Machu Picchu early and rested. - Happy touring..
label: neutro

text_clean: If you come here I recommend camping. There's an entrance fee into the park, but the camping is free. You'll be greeted by donkeys, horses and cows who graze in the area and love the interaction. The views are spectacular at the campsite and the sky is usually clear at night which makes for perfect stargazing. There's a local family that lives up there that'll sell snacks and drinks at slightly higher prices than regular, but nothing too expensive. There's plenty of hiking and climbing to do there with spectacular views at every step. The hike to Laguna 69 is well worth the two hour hike. Take sunscreen though as the sun can get pretty brutal. Also, prepare for an hour+ of unpaved road to get there. Don't let that deter you this place is one of the best spots in Peru!

text_clean: Nunca habia ido a la Reserva y me pareció muy lindo todo. Asistí con unos sobrinos argentinos de 12 y 9 años, al explicarles lo q era una reserva comenzaron a recolectar vasitos, envolturas y más. Lamentablemente mi sobrina piso algo punzante de metal q gracias a Dios no se lo clavó. Fuí a hacerle saber a los guardias y me explicaron q todos los dias limpian las playas pero q la gente no colabora eso es muy triste. Me dijeron q vaya al museo q alli me darian unos polos por colaborar con la reserva, los chicos entusiasmados quisieron ir pero lamentablemente ya no tenian polos y les regalaron un librito para pintar y desarrollar unos laberintos, les dieron también esas sintas donde se cuela el alcohol, mascarillas y un toma todo de metal muy lindo. Todo rotulado con nombre de la reserva. Felices nos retiramos del museo q por cierto es muy interesante. Gracias por alentar a los niños y motivarlos a cuidar el medio ambiente!!

text_clean: CORRUPTION ALERT! Important: this review is NOT about Machu Picchu itself. Machu Picchu is extraordinary and deserves every bit of its reputation. This review is about the management of the ticketing system and the openly tolerated corruption surrounding it. The current circuit reservation system is a mess. We planned our trip months in advance and specifically wanted Circuit 2. We checked availability online repeatedly, including several months before arrival, and it appeared unavailable. We accepted that and adjusted our plans. Then we arrived in Peru and discovered that there were hundreds of tickets available locally for the very circuits that had supposedly been unavailable online. If tickets exist, they should be visible and accessible through the official system. If they are being intentionally reserved for local sales, that should be clearly explained. The lack of transparency makes the entire process feel arbitrary and unfair. What happened inside Machu Picchu was even worse. The site management constantly emphasizes the importance of circuit controls, visitor limits, and strict regulations to protect the monument. Yet while standing near entrances to other circuits, we were approached multiple times by guides who openly suggested they could get us access in exchange for extra cash. We did not ask for this. We were not looking for loopholes. The offers came to us. Not once. Not twice. Multiple times. The most disturbing part was how normal it seemed. There was no hesitation, no attempt to hide it, and guides appeared to be communicating with one another. It gave the impression that everyone knows it is happening and nobody cares enough to stop it. So what exactly is the purpose of all these strict regulations if they can be bypassed with a cash payment? Visitors are told that access restrictions are essential to prevent overcrowding and protect one of the world’s most important archaeological sites. Yet at the same time, people are openly offering ways around those restrictions for money. That is not conservation. That is not heritage protection. That is a system that risks undermining its own credibility. Machu Picchu deserves better than this. The people responsible for managing access should focus less on creating confusing ticketing rules and more on addressing the corruption that visitors can see with their own eyes. If employees, guides, or contractors are openly soliciting unofficial payments at one of the world’s most famous cultural sites, that should be treated as a serious problem and investigated immediately. The monument is incredible. The management experience was deeply disappointing.
label: neutro

text_clean: A Timeless Sacred Experience at Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary, Peru If you’re chasing awe-inspiring scenery woven with ancient wonder, Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary is a must-see. This UNESCO-listed wonder blends breathtaking Andean peaks, remarkable Inca architecture, and a sense of sacred mystery that lingers long after your visit. What makes it special Iconic, panoramic setting: Nestled high in the Andes, the terraced citadel rises against dramatic skies, offering vistas that feel almost otherworldly and utterly unforgettable. Architectural ingenuity: The precision of Inca stonework, the sun temples, and the agricultural terraces showcase sophisticated engineering and aesthetic harmony with the natural landscape. Rich historical context: The sanctuary anchors a wealth of stories about Inca civilization, daily life, astronomy, and ritual practice, inviting exploration and imagination. Spiritual resonance: For many visitors, the site carries a contemplative energy—an invitation to pause, reflect, and connect with history, culture, and nature. Cultural and natural blend: The combination of rocky outcrops, cloud-draped ridges, and well-preserved ruins creates a holistic experience of humanity’s ancient ingenuity in harmony with the environment. What to expect A multi-sensory journey: The textures of stone, the scent of the highland air, and the expansive views create a memorable, immersive adventure. Guided and self-guided insights: Local guides offer fascinating context, while well-marked trails allow for independent exploration at your own pace. Photographic opportunities everywhere: Every terrace, stair, and stonework promises a picture-perfect moment against the dramatic Andean backdrop. Practical tips Plan ahead for altitude and weather: The high elevation can be challenging; hydrate, pace yourself, and dress in layers. Wear sturdy footwear: Expect uneven paths, steps, and some steep sections. Start early or late: To beat crowds and catch the golden light, consider early morning or late afternoon visits. Respect local customs and preservation rules: Stay on designated paths, do not touch ancient walls, and follow guidance to protect this sacred site. Bottom line Machu Picchu’s Historic Sanctuary is a monumental, life-changing destination that blends natural beauty, architectural prowess, and cultural heritage into a once-in-a-lifetime experience. It’s a must-visit for travelers seeking inspiration, historical insight, and a powerful reminder of humanity’s enduring curiosity and resilience. Highly recommended for history buffs, hikers, photographers, and anyone who wants to connect with a sacred echo of the past.
label: neutro

text_clean: It's Machu Picchu, what else is there to say? Beautiful photo locations, great place to see llamas up close. (A local worker informed me they were llamas, and not Alpacas) We did not use a guide service as my wife and I like the solo travel style, but from what I saw the guides were quite knowledgeable and helpful to their tour groups. Questions we had before going there I'll try to answer; 1. What should we wear? We ended up wearing casual hiking clothes. I wore a tank top, t-shirt, into hoodie with just normal pants on the bottom with work/hiking boots. My wife wore a full winter layer setup as she gets cold easily. We both had to take our top layers off as we were in the sun and got hot. Other people looked to be wearing full blown hiking gear (maybe they were doing the Gran Caverna hike or ascending to Machu Picchu without a bus) while others wore more fashionable choices for photos. Honestly the weather will decide it for it and usually mountain climates are unpredictable! 2. Which circuit should we take? We took circuit 3 and found it very nice. Can't speak much for the other two, but from what we saw online circuit 3 had the best views. 3. How can we get a bus ticket in advance? We can't answer that, we ended up buying our ticket in the office the day before our trip at the office. The tickets are for anytime, but are linked to your passport so be sure to bring it to when you buy your ticket(s). Also when you line up for the bus, you'll be organized by your entrance ticket time into Machu Picchu. The bus ride was maybe ~20 minutes, and they send a few at a time up and down. Seems frequent enough. Final Comments There is a toilet at the top but not inside the sanctuary itself. Be sure to go before you go! It's 2 Soles to use the restroom. I found the snack bar at the top to be quite nice and affordable, which is abnormal for such a touristy place. So that was nice! Pack water! Stay hydrated!

text_clean: Negotiated a private taxi to the site from Cusco as the bus fare for 5 people isn't that much of a difference to warrant the extra time travelling. Market selling all kinds of souvenirs at the entrance to the site. Tickets should be purchased in Cusco prior to your visit. Easiest option is to buy them as part of a package at Sacsayhuaman, the fabulous Incan and Pre-Incan site in the hills above Cusco. The site is fairly compact but you should allow at least 3 hours for your visit. The site is not suitable for anyone with walking difficulties. There are marked pathways to follow and handrails to help those people who struggle with heights. The Temple of the Sun at the very top of the site, made of Red Porphory stone speaks volumes as to the age of the Pre-Incan occupation. These stones are massive and incredibly weatherproof. The fact that you can only just make out the Chakhana engravings on the surface of these stones only confirms the fact that they have been in situ for thousands of years, not centuries as the guide books will tell you. The actual Incan ruins are also very impressive. The town itself is worth an overnight stay with accommodation and plenty of places to eat. You can, as an option, catch the tourist train from Cusco to Ollantaytambo as it's on the line to Machu Picchu. Toilet facilities are available inside the site. Child friendly enough but keep an eye on them as some of the drops are sheer.
label: neutro

text_clean: Ollantaytambo is a destination that combines historical intrigue with a bit of a physical challenge. The ascent involves a somewhat demanding staircase with quite a few steps. While it's not exceptionally high, be prepared for the climb. The entrance to Ollantaytambo is conveniently included in the partial ticket that covers four significant sites: Ollantaytambo, Moray, Pisac, and Chinchero. These are the typical inclusions in a one-day Sacred Valley tour, the ticket is not included in the tour and it costs 79 soles. If you're a student or a minor, you'll be eligible for the general ticket, which grants access to many more sites. However, if you're traveling with a group or on a tight schedule, you might not be able to fully take advantage of it. You can find tours starting at 100 soles or opt for private tours around 180 soles. I recommend purchasing the tour once you're in Cusco, as they tend to be more affordable than booking online, where they can go for around $70, which is almost double the cost. The main difference is the quality of the food. The tour I paid for was approximately 120 soles, and while the food wasn't exceptional, it was decent. Our guide recommended staying in ollantaytambo (which is almost at the end of the tour) to facilitate an early morning departure to Machu Picchu since it's much closer to the Ollantaytambo train station. The journey takes about an hour and a half, allowing you to arrive at Machu Picchu early and return later. In my case, I had already booked my round trip to Cusco, but I think is a good advise for people who is planning on going soon. Overall, Ollantaytambo has awesome views and an amazing history behind, I’m pretty sure you’ll enjoy, you definitely need a tour guide to go here, otherwise you won’t enjoy it as much. You can also buy souvenirs here.
label: positivo

text_clean: The actual canyon is breathtaking, this rating is for the local tourism board. We spent a couple of days in Colca Canyon, and all of a sudden on the second day there was a road block where they demanded 70 soles per person tourism tax. We argued with the guy that if you want to charge tourists extortionate amounts of money just to drive through the canyon, you need to let people know before they enter so that one can make an informed decision. I don't really have a problem with paying, I have a problem with the dishonesty. When I complained that there wasn't even a sign, the guy ran around his little hut and got the sign out. He also didn't speak a single word of English, despite the fact that his job is literally to only deal with foreign tourists (locals don't have to pay). When I asked what the money was for, we were told that we get a paper map, and tourist infrastructure. I don't need a paper map, and usually tourist infrastructure is built so that tourists come into the area and then spend money. We will have spent a hell of a lot of money in accomodation, food, car hire, fuel, souvenirs, ... all of which benefits the local economy and through taxes the local government. Why are we asked to pay extra when they don't even provide free public toilets anywhere, and without being information of the cost before entering?

text_clean: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. From around bus terminal different taxi collectivo depart when full, a bit more expensive than a bus, less comfortable but faster. There's only one direct bus from Cabanaconde to Puno at 9am (Nativa Trips, 180 soles). Later you need to get to Chivay (Trasandino, Andalucía or taxi collectivo) from where also only Nativa Trips departs to Puno (1.15pm, 130 soles, stops at few viewpoints on the way). Agencies offer transport to Puno as a full day trip with a lunch (not included) and a few stops on the way. The prices starts from $45-50. If you need to store a luggage in Cabanaconde you can do that in Pachamama Home (10 soles per bag per day).

input_modelo: aspecto: alojamiento reseña: The canyon is amazing. It took us 2.5 hours to get from mirador de San Miguel to San Juan de Chuccho, another 35 minutes to Coshnirhua, 15 minutes to Malata and an hour to Sangalle. We are fit and walk quite fast. Signs are missing in some points or aren't clear. Google Maps shows the route correctly with some exceptions (once or twice there welas a shorter way). I planned to take a shortcut to Sangalle and skip Coshnirhua and Malata. However, when I was in the only place the way supposed to split up (and Google Maps confirmed it) I took unsigned road and after 15 minutes I got to some farmers houses. One of them told me I need to go back so I don't know how to find a shortcut. The next day we went from Sangalle back to Cabanaconde. It took us 2 hours till the mirador, all the way up, and another 20 minutes to Cabanaconde. The trial isn't long but quite demanding. I recommend to start early to avoid walking in sun. Remember that admission fee is 70 soles for tourist from out of Latin America, 40 soles if you're from Latin America and there are some discount for students and Peruvians. There are some check points so don't throw away. Trasandino goes from Arequipa to Chivay (9am, 11.45am & 10.30pm) from where you need another bus or taxi collectivo and Andalucía goes directly to Cabanaconde (6.30am, 9.30am, 1.30pm & 10pm; 25 soles). Agencies like Perou Voyage also offer a transport - more expensive but from your accommodation to Cabanaconde with few stops. text_clean: I visited the archaeological site today, and although the tour was interesting, I was shocked by the living standards of the animals on the site, present to illustrate the resources of the ancient people. There were ducks with no access to fresh water, which is part of their essential living environment - instead they had a stagnant, filthy puddle and were surrounded by their own waste. The guinea pigs were also living in tiny pens, covered in their own waste. I would ask the site management to please rethink the provisions they are giving to these animals - perhaps even charging a slightly higher ticket price to allow for a better environment. The current living standards are really not appropriate, and lets down an otherwise good value tour.

text_clean: Justo lo visite un fin de semana, un domingo que fue primer domingo de junio. Grande fue mi sorpresa al saber que esos domingos son de entrada gratis. Cómo llegué con mi vehículo temprano pude entrar y estacionarme con facilidad, sin embargo me indicaron que podía hacer el recorrido en mi vehículo El camino tiene varias paradas para poder salir a ver las ruinas. Debo decir que todo sigue igual en cuanto al estado del complejo, pues yo esperaba que después de casi 10 años de haberlo visitado nuevamente al menos hubiesen podido mostrar la restauración del muro pintado. Tampoco me dejó muy satisfecho el recorrido por las afueras del templo pues no se puede apreciar nada, solo es darle una vuelta y mirar un muro de barro. Aunque me dijeron que por el temblor no dejaban ingresar pues podía haber quedado algunos adobes inestables Lo más rescatable es su museo de sitio donde tiene restos muy vistosos. También hay una cafetería un poco más caro que el promedio son los precios. Tenemos además la tienda de recuerdos. Y los servicios higiénicos están muy limpios y bien cuidados.

text_clean: Note that while the site itself is pretty, the logistics of getting tickets is very hard to plan for. Up until a few months ago you can get the following day ticket if you waited outside the ticket office before it open. Not sure exactly when it changed but instead of let’s say arriving in the evening in aguas calliente on day one getting a ticket on day two for day three to head to Machu Picchu, now you have to plan for an extra day on top of that. So day one you arrive in town, get tickets and the next two days are sold out, day two and three you wait in town day 4 you head to Machu Picchu. The ticket office is located in aguas calliente so you are essentially stuck there several days. The government is unreliable and may change their policy again. Making figuring out the timing exceptionally difficult if you haven’t bought Machu Picchu tickets online like many months ahead of time. Because of this, I do not advise buying nonrefundable airplane tickets or hotels or tours for any part of the vacation happening after Machu Picchu. In fact I would advise giving yourself 2 extra wiggle room days just to account for Machu Picchu scheduling uncertainty. Note that bus tickets to Machu Picchu from town are also only good for three days. Especially with all the hoop jumping and the logistical corrections that had to take place because of their inconsistent ticketing system. Like Pompeii is also an ancient city. No exploitation there and it was a lot more enjoyable experience. Get in, see Pompeii, get out and then move on. You can’t do that here. One can’t help but feel all of it was orchestrated as a giant tourist trap. I don’t think there is any other place where you are forced to stay in a very isolated town for four days just so you can see a ruin.

text_clean: Waste of time. You need to buy tickets and you have to pay in cash. There are no signs that say you need to buy tickets. There are no signs that say you have to pay in cash. This is operated by the government, right? How is it possible for them to do such a bad job with this site? There were 2 more people after me who had the same problem. I will probably never get another chance to see this because these people are too lazy to put up a sign and too incompetent to just by something to swipe your card in. I got a burger from a hole in the wall place that took my card, but they cant take cards here? I tried to buy tickets in advance but the website didn't work. It's just insane to me that they cant take a card and you basically have to climb a mountain for some guy to tell you that you're not allowed in. I'm very angry. This really messed up my trip to Cusco and I don't think I want to come back here because of it. You literally have to climb a mountain to get here. The government of Peru sucks.

text_clean: El museo de chan chan es un lugar accesorio a las ruinas de chan chan, donde la entrada viene incluida con la ciudad de barro. No hay que contratar guía aunque ellos te lo recomienden (para sacar dinero). Dentro del museo encontrarás distintos objetos y arte de esta cultura. Tienen una sala con una proyección y una maqueta gigante en el suelo en modo interactivo que debes pedir que enciendan (ya que si no lo pides no lo ponen), en ella se observa como fue la evolución de la ciudad y el alcance de su expansión (merece la pena, de verdad). Tienen perros calatos (peruanos) en la entrada y suelen entrar por el museo. Los del museo se negaban a poner la protección para ahorrarse el dinero y el trabajo, por eso mi valoración tan baja. Hay que exigirles que lo pongan para verlo. No dejes de aprovechar tu entrada en la ciudad para ver este museo.
label: neutro

text_clean: All in all, what you see from the outside is what you get. As Canadians visiting in February, we made the grave error of visiting during the heat of the day. This place is a hot hellscape on a Peruvian summer day. Only accessible during a tour, the location offers no respite from the sun. The tour takes a full hour, which, while very interesting and educational, is a bit of a long time to be looking at bricks. Our tour guide was very good, and we enjoyed learning about Lima culture, but there isn't much to see other than the clay mud bricks and the views from the top, which are definitely quite good. If you plan to visit, go during the evening.

text_clean: Kuelap es un bonito destino, pero muy atrás de Machu Picchu, Ollantaytambo o Choquequirao. Todavía es complicado llegar, no hay aeropuerto en una ciudad cercana, y la infraestructura y los servicios para llegar al lugar son aun pobres y riesgosos.

text_clean: This was a site visited as part of our large tour group that was in town for a few days en route to Machu Picchu. Saqsaywaman was interesting and it is very impressive in terms of the size of the stones and the precision of the placement but I must admit to being somewhere underwhelmed after that, there isn't much else to do here.... aside from visit the large Jesus statue on the way up the hill. If you have the chance to choose between this and touring the beautiful palace in Cusco, you may want to choose the palace.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: The content of the museum is amazing and some of the best I've seen in South America. Unfortunately the museum is massively let down by the lack of story and content conveyed by the information boards. Also none of the information on the second and third, the lowest level, has any translation into other languages and the top level only into English (about 25% of the whole museum) so its pointless going if you want to learn and not just see pretty objects. I believe they have the option of a guide to squeeze more money from you if you like. I'd recommend reading up beforehand so you know what your going to see.

text_clean: The fountains are very nice and it could be a relaxing and enjoyable park. The park staff and police are trying to get a handle on the informal(illegal) venders in the park. Which for the most part are not that big of a deal, but when you are watching the fountain and light show and there is some guy repeatedly yelling out whatever he is selling and throwing things around to'advertise' it gets old real quick. All the police and park staff do is stand there with them. They don't actually remove them.

text_clean: Stopping locally so went in the morning (great time to go, as I was leaving at 1400 lots of minibuses had arrived and it was getting busy) I had the templo del sol all to myself for 15 minutes. Fantastic place with breathtaking views. After that I found half of it was closed off, maybe even more than half. Not a problem, if I'd been told when I came in and the main route map was still showing two routes you can't do. The place is fantastic, what I could walk round, and well worth a visit, but I'd be lying if I wasn't disappointed about the more than half I didn't know you couldn't walk round. Extra star back on for the water temple area to the right when you get in. This place is so well presented and relaxing I used the spare time I had from not being able to see most of the site to sit, relax, take in the view and read my book. A good visit but a little disappointing.
label: neutro

text_clean: Rating based only on the hike from Cabanaconde down to the oasis and back. The walk is doable in a day but made unpleasant by a deteriorating path and aggressive horsemen leading pack animals among it. Start early as there is no shelter between top and bottom. The oasis was clearly once beautiful, but is now marred by current and failed development attempts. Rubbish disposal appears to be a real issue. For the 70 soles entry fee I would hope better care can be taken of this area going forward.
label: positivo

text_clean: 1) YOU CANNOT WALK BACK, OR REPEAT ANY PART OF YOUR CIRCUIT. There are staff watching and they will blow their whistle and shout at you if you try. We arrived in a downpour so we walked to some shelter and waited it out. When we tried to walk back on the circuit we had paid for, we were not allowed. It doesn't matter that it's not busy, you want to take photos or any other reason, they will not let you walk back, even 20m. Unfortunately this was not clear when we purchased the ticket so we missed a significant portion of our route :( Therefore I'd advise you to walk slowly and enjoy the views, because once you arrive at the exit, that will be the end of your time at macchu pichu. I feel the staff lack compassion since it was 7am and quiet at macchu pichu, and I didn't want it to rain. If they had a cafe inside at the beginning I'd have waited there, but you have to enter at your time slot so I was left in a downpour in my poncho. I'd paid for flights, accomodation, and so many buses and trains to be there. The staff didn't show any compassion for my situation and were curt. 2) TOILETS WILL COST YOU EXTRA ON TOP OF THE ENTRANCE FEE, AND BAGGAGE STORAGE. Which I also find ridiculous considering the entrance ticket is so expensive. Toilet S/2 and baggage is S/5. 3) YOU CANNOT ENTER BEFORE THE ENTRANCE TIME ON YOUR TICKET Something to do with the system they use. 4) DO NOT FLY A DRONE The staff have a walkie talkie system and respond very quickly. I don't know what the consequences are, but the people we saw were taken to the office. 5) DO NOT BOOK EARLY MORNING TIME SLOT IF YOU ARE VISITING IN WET/ SHOULDER SEASONS The clouds tend to hang low in the sky in the morning so you might not have the best view/ you might have to wait for clouds to clear. 6) YOU CAN BUY THE BUS TICKET FOR THE BUS UPWARDS ONLINE, BEFOREHAND (12 USD per ride) Just look for the one bus company that runs the route. I can't remember off the top of my head. You can also purchase them at the bottom and at the top, the process is quick and easy. Hope this helps :) I did route 1&2 and was happy with all the areas it covered.

text_clean: Espectacular sitio de conexión entre la naturaleza y las familias que visitan el lugar. Desde el estacionamiento con 780msnm Inicia el ascenso por un camino adaptado y compatible con el entorno por un espacio de 15 min aprox, pasando por 2 puentes de madera sobre la misma quebrada del Ahuashiyacu. Llegas al lugar donde se encuentra la poza bajo la caída del agua con 900 msnm aprox. Ideal para estar con la familia y amigos, el lugar tiene clima templado de 18-22 Centígrados. Lo que hace muy agradable la estancia. Asimismo la vista y el espectáculo del agua (formas y sonidos) conectan La Paz del medio. Recomiendo visita * * * * * 👍🏻

text_clean: There's an entry gate at the start of the climb with no signs indicating this is a paid activity. The ticket office is right at the top and entry price is 70 soles ($20) per person. Clearly relying on a sunk cost approach of unprepared people doing this climb at high altitude and not wanting it to be for nothing. The ruins look great and impressive but dishonest price gouging is simply ugly. We decided not to enter and walk around instead. That's 80% of the experience right there. Charge less or be clear at the entry to the climb that there is a charge so that people can make an informed decision beforehand.

text_clean: Beautiful site, which we wanted to visit with a guide to learn more about the history of the site and the meanings of the “rocks”. There are several guides at the entrance for an additional fee. However, if you go with a guide, first check his/her belief on how the Inca’s built the place. Ours was convinced it was built by aliens and ufo’s. No kidding. It took us a while to realize she was very serious about it. It was funny on one hand, but was kind of a waste of money on the other hand.

text_clean: You can’t beat the price for this attraction. What could use vast improvement is the mandatory guided tour, at least the guide we were paired with. The amplification system used was not adequate for the number of people in our group. But the worst was the scripted, lacklustre delivery of the tour. Our guide spoke in a monotone, never deviated from the script and hardly ever engaged with the crowd. Her comment at the end about tips being appreciated was almost laughable. I didn’t see any money change hands. The museum should increase the entrance fee and invest in better guides who add value to this incredible site.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo en cuanto a objetos y diseño es un 10/10. PERO llegué 430pm, 1era vez que visito el museo, cuando me vendieron las entradas, nunca me dijeron q la hora de cierre estaba próxima. Después cuando estaba dentro disfrutando del museo, alguien del museo me apuró, me dijo "en 1 hr se ve todo, ajustando 45min". Ir a un museo es una experiencia, sobre todo un museo con tantos detalles como este. No pude leer ni admirar el 50% de los objetos. Cada objeto tiene un texto bastante detallado además de ser obras muy bien trabajadas, obras q uno necesita procesar con tiempo, admirar. Soy de Lima, no sé cuando regrese, lamentablemente no tuve una buena experiencia. Sí antes de venderme la entrada me hubieran dicho "señor estamos próximos a cerrar, tome en cuenta q es probable que el tiempo no le sea suficiente" ya sabría a lo q me estaba lanzando. Sí van a ir, calculen 2 hrs para admirar todo, para procesar el arte de cada obra y no ir al museo solo por entrar y ya.

text_clean: To be honest, before we got to Machu Picchu, I thought of it as a tourist trap of sorts, but that was very presumptuous of me. In reality, it is an AMAZING place, full of history and culture. It is beautiful and majestic, and puts one in awe of the genius of the ancient people, who had no writing, no benefit of modern tools, but were able to create such impressive structures that withstood time and nature. It is a very spiritual place, surrounded by incredible views of the mountains and the forest (and a bit of a tourist trap :) Definitely, you MUST have a knowledgeable guide who can tell you about the traditions of the ancient Incas, explain the architecture and provide a proper background story. We were very lucky to have such a guide. His name is Jimmy. He was very professional, was able to answer all of our questions and was great at taking pictures – he knew all the best spots and angles. He conducted his tour in English and demonstrated deep knowledge of the historical significance of the Machu Picchu site and the surrounding area. We took the bus up from the Machu Picchu Village to the archeological site and back. There is a pedestrian trail that one can take, they estimate about 2 hours up and about an hour down. It may be a good idea, but keep in mind that if the sun is out, it gets pretty hot going up the mountain. The site is divided into several Circuits. We bought tickets for the 2B Circuit, which allowed us to go up to the Guardian House and enjoy the views along the way, and then, access to the village, the king’s palace and the Temple of the Sun. This route offered magnificent views of the mountains and the Machu Picchu site, as well as a close up look at the buildings and passages within the site. I highly recommend this particular route. Our trip to Peru was a last minute, spontaneous decision, so we did not pre book our tickets to the Machu Picchu site. There was no way to purchase advance tickets, as they stopped being available about three months in advance. So, we decided to try our luck and proceed to the site hoping to get tickets there. Each day the Machu Picchu administration releases 1,000 tickets to Machu Picchu that can be purchase in person at the ticket office in Machu Picchu Village. A few days before we got there, they changed the way they process the ticket sales, which works out much better for those who wish to buy the tickets on site. That is, once ALL the 1,000 tickets are sold for the next day, they release 1,000 tickets for the following day, and so on. This is what we did: we arrived in Ollantaytambo from Cusco by a private car, and spent a night there. Then we took a train to the Machu Picchu Village and arrived there around 2 pm. We immediately went to the ticket office. By that time, they were selling tickets for the day after. We were able to book 2B Circuit, which is considered the best route through the archeological site. Once we had our tickets, we booked our return train to Ollantaytambo. So we ended up spending two nights in Machu Picchu Village, had our tour at 11 am and left on the train the same day, later in the afternoon. Please be advised that the train tickets you purchase from Ollantaytambo to the Machu Picchu Village (expensive as they are) ARE NOT the site entrance tickets. Those MUST be purchased separately. We witnessed a few people at the ticket office realizing that they booked all their train rides, but in fact, they did not have the site entry tickets and had to re-book their return train, because there were no Machu Picchu tickets available for the day they planned. Make sure you wear layers, because it may get pretty hot up at the Machu Picchu site. I would recommend a hat and insist on sunscreen. Finally, I can’t stress enough how important it is to have a competent guide while visiting the archeological site, it makes the experience complete.
label: positivo

text_clean: Amazing place, this MAGIC CIRCUT OF WATER is a place where everyone can come and enjoy the beautiful flora, fauna and water on a daily evening show. This is the #1 icon of the city of LIMA. It has 13 gigantic water 💧 cybernetic fountains mixed with high technology where MUSIC, SOUNDS & LASER lights are mixed together to present a show that is unique to the Peruvian people and the world 🌎😀. We experienced an exciting and unforgettable show that was full of Colors, Music, Light and Magic. We were Very much enchanted and surprised by this great cybernetic show. This show is for all ages. We are so happy that our body guard suggested this attraction, thank you my friend and thank you to all the people from LIMA, PERU 🇵🇪😀 that have taken the time to talk to us and show us the great beauty of their country. Thank you GOD for giving us this amazing opportunity 🙏, AMEN. SINCERELY, TONY MORA 🙏❤💙♥.

text_clean: Para ir primero, toman carro en la terminal hacia kuelap que está 7 soles. Luego toman el teleferico que está 20,40 soles por persona que incluye ida y vuelta en la couster y teleférico 🚡 y que solo lo pueden usar hasta la hora que te indican, sino deberás volver a pagar los 20,40 soles para regresar. Al llegar deben pagar 30 soles por persona adulta para ingresar a kuelap, y finalmente subir a kuelap que es 30 minutos de caminata hacia arriba. De bajada es más rápido. A mi opinión personal, pienso que está sobrevalorado el costo de ingresar, ya que hay miradores con árboles de eucalipto y frutos en el camino, donde la entrada es 3 soles y que están cerca, asimismo podrían ir a las cataratas de gocta más temprano o los miradores de huancas y uno más que no me acuerdo. La verdad, que más me emocionó subir al teleferico 🚡 que llegar a kuelap, que fue la única decepción de mi viaje, además que ya no permiten ingresar a las casitas para tomar foto. Antes se podía según lo que me contó una persona que ya visitaba el lugar por segunda vez. Olvidaba, para ir a kuelap NO TOMEN GUIADO NI TOUR. no es necesario, es fácil de llegar.
label: negativo

text_clean: De reserva solo tiene el nombre y lo que te cobran en la entrada. Apenas hay carteles informativos sobre las especies y su estado de conservación, época de cría o paso por la reserva. Hay pescadores pescando con red, varias personas retirando las algas de la playa ( no sé si es por turismo o pesca) que son ecosistemas super importantes para mucha fauna que vive en ellas. Las carreteras dan lástima, los carros y bugys se meten en la playa y por las dunas ( donde viven y anidan muchas especies) y para colmo el centro de interpretación está cerrado por "mantenimiento" . El baño tampoco está incluido en el precio de la entrada. Una vergüenza de reserva, solo es un saca dinero para los turistas. Los paisajes y las playas son muy bonitas y hay muchas aves en la zona
label: negativo

text_clean: Es un lugar hermoso y de visita obligada como turista. Queda a 30 minutos de la ciudad, en la cordillera La Escalera. La entrada es de 6 soles. Para acceder no hay opción para ir con silla de ruedas, el camino es de 15 minutos de subida de escaleras y es más seguro si se coge de la baranda. Tiene dos niveles, donde hay y no hay piso. Recomiendo entrar con zapatillas de baño. El agua es helada, pero refrescante. Hay servicios higiénicos y lugares para cambiarse. En la catarata los guías dan recomendaciones con megáfonos, para estar protegidos (gracias por hacerlo).
label: positivo

Recomiendo entrar con zapatillas de baño. El agua es helada, pero refrescante. Hay servicios higiénicos y lugares para cambiarse. En la catarata los guías dan recomendaciones con megáfonos, para estar protegidos (gracias por hacerlo).

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: El museo está cerrado y los restos reales de la tumba de Sipán no se pueden visitar. Según el personal, hay problemas con el aire acondicionado y tardarán un año en volver a abrir completamente al público. Se pueden visitar tres espacios con réplicas y algunos hallazgos interesantes, pero si vienes a Lambayeque a ver al “Tutankamon peruano” te irás de aquí sin verlo. Es una lástima que el Ministerio de Cultura haya dejado un espacio como este en condiciones tan precarias y que no facilite la información con antelación. Me voy decepcionado y esperando que en el futuro se tomen en serio la protección y muestra de la rica cultura arqueológica de Lambayeque.
label: negativo

text_clean: It might sound insane to rate this site as 2 star consider is a world heritage site but this was one of the most deflating experiences of my trip. We got the circuito 1 ticket which basically keeps you out of the site and you get is a view. AND NOTICE ONCE UP THERE YOU CANNOT BUY A SECOND TICKET . When we realized this we tried to get another ticket but we were told we needed to go down to the town and buy another one and then go back up again. Which means a couple of hours by bus and your train tickets might be lost as they also have set times. To be honest they should not sell this ticket! thank God they sun came out and we had a site other wise we would have spend $300 to see a cloud! I really dislike how artificial and turistic this attraction is and how circuito 1 feels more like a scam

text_clean: What a waste of time! You go there and get tickets without a wait. The guide at the entrance who ended up being our guide during the tour told us the tour is mandatory and we have to tip her at the end. She repeated the tip part twice more during our tour but she was definitely bored and only interested in the money cause she would give us literally 1minute of basic info and then tell us to take photos while she walked on checking her phone for the entire hour! The info she gave us were marginally more than the info you read at the little room next to the ticket office and there was no explanation or contextualisation. Anyways, I felt it was a waste of time (the tour lasts one hour) and a shame she did not help us understand and appreciate more the ancient Lima culture. I was left confused because others have written amazing reviews so maybe it was just this lady guide that was really bad.

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Un museo muy bonito, sin embargo es muy frustrante que no te dejen tomar fotos dentro de este. Tiene tres pisos, empiezas por el tercero y vas bajando, te muestran no sólo la tumba del señor de sipan, sino también otras tumbas que se encontraron dentro de la misma huaca, sus métodos de sepultura, la cerámica de los mochica y su cosmovisión (dioses).

destination: Museo Tumbas Reales del Señor de Sipán
text_clean: Es difícil puntuar como corresponde al Museo porque hay varios factores para tener en cuenta. Para empezar, cuando una entra al estacionamiento, tiene que volver a la entrada para adquirir el ingreso y el valor nos pareció más bien de índole simbólica * 10 soles para adultes * 4 soles para adultes mayores y estudiantes *1.50 soles niñes. Ingresamos en subida al mismo, y me quedé pensando, si la subida es tan empinada, ¿Cómo hacen para ingresar las personas con movilidad reducida o usuaries de silla de ruedas? Adentro del museo, el calor, hacia mitad del recorrido se hace realmente muy pesado, sumándole a la experiencia el uso obligatorio que tenemos que hacer como corresponde de la mascarilla. La exposición entera, está muy bien armada, sólo a veces (muy pocas en realidad) el relato construido para guiar la visita cambia de persona, al principio se mantiene con una "objetividad" y luego pasa a una primera persona colectiva. A tener en cuenta algunos de los termómetros no funcionan (: Es la cuarta vez que viajo hasta Lambayeque para poder compartir esta belleza de museo, en esta ocasión, fui acompañada de tres personas más y resulta que la última sala que es realmente la frutilla en el postre. Casi que el impulso principal para ver tan increíble sala viva, resulta que está inhabilitada, cerrada por covid. Lo cual, es ilógico debido a que estamos dentro del museo y esa sala, no se diferencia en exposición del resto de las salas. Así que, sigue siendo recomendable, pero deberían reactivarla.
label: positivo

text_clean: This is a great place to visit, I would say it is a must if you are visiting Lima. And if you are a resident in the Peruvian capital, it is a must visit every so often. The Park where the Magic Water Circuit is located is huge (8 hectares) and opens from 3:00 p.m. to 10:30 p.m. It is not only a park for the enjoyment of children, but for the whole family. It is very well preserved, located very centrally, and the prices (tickets) are very accessible: PEN 4.00 per person. It is a circuit of 13 computer-controlled fountains, which offer a fun, interesting and unique show of music, color, images ... and water. Do not miss it. The fountain that displays videos on a water screen, has "functions" at 19:15, 20:15 and 21:30, so you should try to be in front of it at that time ... or a little earlier since many of the almost 10,000 daily visitors that the park receives on average are crowded together for the show. ________________________ Este es un gran lugar para visitar, yo diría que imperdible si es que estás de visita en Lima, y si eres residente en la capital peruana, pues es de obligada visita cada cierto tiempo. El Parque donde está ubicado el Circuito Mágico del Agua es enorme (8 hectáreas) y abre desde la 15:00 hasta las 22:30. No solo es un parque para el disfrute de los niños, sino de toda la familia. Está muy bien conservado, ubicado muy céntricamente, y los precios (entradas) son muy accesibles: PEN 4.00 por persona. Se trata de un circuito de 13 fuentes controladas por computadora, las cuales ofrecen un divertido, interesante y único espectáculo de música, color, imágenes… y agua. No te lo pierdas. La fuente que despliega videos en un screen de agua, tiene “funciones” a las 7:15, 8:15 y 9:30, por lo que debes tratar de estar en frente de ella a esas horas… o un poco antes ya que se aglomeran muchos de los casi 10,000 visitantes diarios que el parque recibe en promedio.
label: negativo

text_clean: For me, it's an Overrated marketing success of an abandoned settlement. I will submit that the majestic mountains and valleys are the main draw and attraction. The hyped settlement architecture? It is just a cluster of abandoned stone/rock/mud low 'buildings' meant for 3-400 families. Sure, some walls are tightly stacked with big chipped/carved boulders/rocks and even a blade can't get thru the gaps... The mountain terrace carved/landscaped for agriculture is not unique to the Inca. The use of gravity to channel water is common amongst other old civilisations too... With a good guide, the story telling of Inca civilisation and the Inca connection to mother earth and nature can be engaging. Know too that getting there to MP is a long journey and time wasting pain, compounded with an eventual convoluted Q system to get expensive tickets. (It can be a 2 day Q for a 3hrs just one section visit of MP). .. And a tourist trap village venue to take a bus up to MP. When there, Also beware the nasty midges/sand flies which can carry the deadly oropouch virus. Only this is free. 🤣😂 Oh, the story of the hacked down obelisk to accommodate the Prez helicopter landing is outlandish.
label: negativo

text_clean: Not worth the time. Feels like a tourist trap replica of what might have been the discovery of a lifetime in the center of Lima. I had much anticipated visiting this sight, only to be met with a forced on guided tour with the world’s worst tour guide. I arrived having not much knowledge of the place and fully expecting to learn at least something how/what/when/why there were ruins in the middle of a metropolis such a Lima, but instead left what was to be an 1 hr guided tour approximately 20 mins into it. Later on, I read some of the Google reviews and it seems I must have had the same tour guide as many of them. She was boring, uninterested and has no place in being a tour guide. I say skip this place as it is currently ran. It’s a shame as I’m sure there is a very interesting fact as to how Huaca Pucllana came to be in the middle of Lima.

text_clean: Te va a gustar el sitio arqueológico si haces treaking o te apasiona la culturas peruanas, ya que el recorrido es bastante extenso y agotador. Hay algunas subidas muy elevadas. La vista frente al mar es bellísima. Por otro lado, el museo también es bellísimo, hay bastantes esculturas y herramientas pre incas. SIN EMBARGO, el trato de los guardias y guías que están en el museo de hostigante y ofensivo, puesto que te persiguen si es que vas por tu cuenta mirando y sin una guía, una de ellas me dijo “señorita por favor” y yo “??” Cuando solo estaba mirando sola una parte del museo. Además deberían mejorar el sistema ya que algunas compañeras no las dejaron pasar al museo porque perdieron su ticket. Amigos, ya estamos 2024, hagan un código qr o algo. Eso y que no te dejan entrar con tu mochila ni beber adentro agua. F

text_clean: Beautiful place and I would give it 10 stars. The views are breathtaking, the site is really big and worth seeing. The only thing I didn’t like is that one of the security guys was throwing rocks at lamas and dogs. I understand that they cannot go everywhere and they might destroy something, but why to throw the rocks directly at them and hurt them? I would be more glad (well, I would not, but let it be) if he threw the rocks near the animals to scare them, not aiming directly at them (and he was successful in aiming). The scared lama was running really fast and nearly ran at me. I reported it to the only guy that spoke English at the gate, firstly he laughed a little bit, but he told something to the security guy. Hope it changed something.

text_clean: If you have been in Tikal (Mayans culture - Guatemala) you not gonna feel impressive by Machu Picchu. Also it is really expensive all. Train, bus, entrance. Advices: #1 If you are with your own transport go to Hidroeletrica Machu Picchu, then park your vehicle there and go walk to Aguas Calientes (the closest city to the entrance of Machu Picchu site). You gonna walk around 2 hours but it gonna worth, once is all straight line, and train tickets are so expensive. #2 if you are on tour, you can take a van from Cusco or Urubamba (or even other cities) to Hidroeletrica Machu Picchu, and from there, follow the instructions on tip #1, but take a train from Cusco or wherever it gonna cost you 2837474 months worked. #3 There is no sale tickets at the entrance of the park. Yes, sounds weird. You have option to buy online, with a previous booking or at Aguas Calientes Cultural Centre (in Aguas Calientes). #4 Keep in mind there are 2 entrance open time: 6am and 12pm. But if you buy the tickets today for tomorrow or even for 3 days later may you not find the first period tickets. Once everyone has preference for morning time and wanna watch the sunrise. #5 there is no way to get at Machu Picchu by your own transport. #6 from aguas Calientes to the entrance of the site you gonna climb for 1h. But it worth the view are amazing, otherwise you can sell your kidney and go by bus, $12 going up, $12 going down (consider that 12 fu**ing dollars is lot of money in Peru). I guess that's all... feel free to contact me through here if you need more info about it. :) Ps.: no, I'm not working with tour. I'm just a traveler who wants enjoy the life pay what is fear and worth

text_clean: Centro turístico por excelencia en Perú. Es un lugar con bastante historia. La Plaza Mayor de Lima está bastante iluminado y tiene banquetas donde puedes descansar con total tranquilidad. Alrededor de la Plaza está el Palacio municipal de Lima, la Catedral de Lima y el Palacio de Gobierno. Hay bastante información sobre el lugar en Internet. Mencionar que no ingresan vehículos (autos ni motos) por las calles circundantes, así que es necesario aparcar en lugares más alejados. Hay estacionamientos pagados cercanos. Puedes pasar con bicicletas y a pie. La Plaza está bien conservada y limpia, aunque nunca faltan las personas que ensucian indiscriminadamente. La Plaza es segura, cuenta con vigilancia municipal y policial. Las imágenes son de noviembre 2022.

text_clean: 6-7 hour bus ride from Arequipa (terminal terrestre, Arturo Ibanez 130) to Cabanaconde. Route over highlands, vicuna reserve, down to Chivay in canyon, then down canyon, (best views from right side of bus). 9:30 AM Arequipa departure (Andelusia) and nights in Cabanaconde before and after self guided trek, seemed better than 3:00 AM Arequipa departure, short last trek day and back to Arequipa of tours. Many places to stay, I was at Pachamama hostel about 2 blocks up from the plaza on San Pedro St. Good information about trek options and good place to meet others doing unguided treks. I did 2d/1n trek, 1st day to Llahuar with scenic descent along canyon edge, then down into side canyon, final descent to Colca river crossing, climb into Taruna, back down to Llahuar on the Colca river just after crossing the Huaruro river. Nice bungalows on river, friendly staff, fixed menu dinner, breakfast and sandwichs available, all at very reasonable prices. Natural hot pools and swimming pool right on river not open because river very high in early March. Very ambitious new "restaurant" construction ongoing. As hike to Llahuar mostly down and not too long, I was there well before noon. Could just chill in nice location. I opted for day hike up Huaruro canyon that starts steeply right from Llahuar then more gradually up to Llatica where a bridge crosses the river. From there another steep climb to high trail that contours back down other side of canyon to above Belen. Nice safe and scenic canyon trails. Down main street in Belen past soccer field to steep trail to Paclla. Cross road and continue on trail down through terraced gardens that eventually goes to Taruna above Llahuar. This added another 11 km and 700m vertical, about 4 hours at a fast pace, to first day. 2nd day back up to Belen, options are trail short cuts to Paclla not too steep, then very steep to Belen, or longer but more gradually walk on road. From Belen you are on road up to viewpoint bend then level to shortcut trail down to plateau, join main trail down to Sangalle (relatively touristy and expensive, but amazing given that everything has to come in by horse or burro). That just leaves the 2.5 - 3.5 hour all uphill switchbacks to plateau and final km to Cabanaconde. Total for 2nd day a healthy 16-18km with 1700m elevation gain. If I was doing it again, would spend 2 nights in canyon, 1st at Llahuar, then 2nd up at Fure with day hike up to the waterfall (cataratas), day 3 high trail back from Fure contouring back to join road at high point above Sangalle, then through Sangalle up and out. Another interesting high altitude strenuous option might be continuing up from Fure to Tocallo at 3800m where some maps show a trail that goes in and out of several canyons with final pass then long descent to Malata above Sangalle. Another night in canyon and some local information about condition of trails would be needed. Canyon country was unique, scenic and very green with lots of water in the rainy season. Lodging and meals in many areas down in canyon allows for very light packs, a plus given all the climbs. No reservations needed for anything during rainy season, probably a different story in high season. A minibus service that goes on the not too scary dirt road from Carbanaconde, crossing the Rio Colca at the same place my trek did has beautiful brand new rain sheltered stops at all the little towns all the way to Tapay! This allows some less strenuous options for the canyon that are sure to become more popular.
label: negativo

text_clean: Fuimos a la salida de un curso el día domingo, y esta fue mi segunda vez asistiendo. Sin embargo, por segunda vez consecutiva noté los mismos detalles que deberían mejorar. Los recorridos son demasiado planos y se vuelven algo monótonos, además de que los grupos son muy grandes, lo que hace que no todos los visitantes presten atención o se sientan incluidos. En mi caso, la señorita que guiaba avanzaba muy rápido y parecía dirigirse solo a un grupo reducido de personas, dejando de lado a los demás, sin atender las dudas que teníamos. También avanzaba a su propio ritmo, sin esperar que el resto del grupo se reagrupe, lo cual me pareció poco considerado. Fuera de esos puntos, el lugar en sí está muy bien cuidado. Se nota el cariño y el buen manejo que tienen, ya que todo se ve limpio, ordenado y en excelente estado. Aunque todo está muy bien conservado, hay ciertas partes como la zona final del recorrido, en la parte más alta de la huaca endonde se sienten algunos tramos un poco peligrosos, sobre todo al descender, ya que hay curvas pronunciadas sin barandas o elementos de seguridad que podrían evitar accidentes. En general, es un lugar valioso y bien preservado, pero el servicio de guiado y la seguridad en algunos tramos podrían mejorar mucho la experiencia del visitante.

destination: Líneas y Geoglifos de Nasca y Palpa
text_clean: Anímate a visitar las enigmáticas Líneas de Nasca Las Líneas de Nasca son unos curiosos geoglifos (figuras construidas en cerros usando piedras). Se encuentran localizadas en las Pampas de Jumana, en el desierto de Nasca, entre esta ciudad y Palpa en Perú. Algunas de las líneas poseen un ancho de entre los 40 y los 210 centímetros y se extienden por 520 km², y otras incluso se prolongan hasta un área de 800 km². El principal atractivo son las Líneas de Nasca, las cuales se pueden ver desde una avioneta o subiendo a las colinas cercanas, dado que estas se han mantenido en buen estado debido al buen clima que tiene la ciudad. Asimismo, puedes visitar museos y los alrededores del lugar. El costo aproximado del pasaje en avioneta bordea los USD 100 dólares por persona, a lo que se le suma unos 30 soles por impuestos aeroportuarios. Conviene hacer el recorrido aéreo durante las primeras horas de la mañana, ya que es cuando suele estar más despejado y hay menos viento, lo que favorecerá tu visibilidad y te permitirá tomar mejores fotos. En promedio, el recorrido dura unos 30 minutos, y en el trayecto podrás observar 12 figuras, entre ellas el mono, la araña, el pelícano, entre otros. No te preocupes mucho por el clima, no por nada Nazca es conocida como «la ciudad del eterno verano». El clima es soleado y seco durante todo el año, con una temperatura promedio de 23°C aunque puede llegar hasta los 30°C entre los meses de enero y marzo. Si vas a sobrevolar las Líneas de Nazca es importante que lleves una casaca cortavientos, pues puede haber vientos huracanados en el desierto. Recuerda que el vuelo dura unos 30 minutos aproximadamente (lleva pastillas para el mareo, por si acaso). Al ser un clima soleado y cálido, no olvides tu bloqueador, lentes de sol y otros objetos “veraniegos”. Si eres de los que temen las turbulencias, te recomendamos volar las Líneas de Nazca por la mañana, y así evitar los fuertes vientos de la tarde. Que te diviertas..!!! Si te gusto la información dale "Like"

text_clean: Machu Picchu is an ancient Incan citadel located high in the Andes Mountains of Peru. The site is one of the most iconic and breathtakingly beautiful destinations in the world, attracting millions of visitors every year. To reach Machu Picchu, visitors can either hike the famous Inca Trail or take a train from the nearby town of Cusco. Once there, visitors can explore the ruins of the citadel, which include impressive structures such as temples, terraces, and plazas. The intricate stonework and engineering feats of the Incas are truly awe-inspiring. While Machu Picchu can get crowded, especially during peak season, there are ways to avoid the crowds and make the most of the experience. One option is to visit early in the morning or later in the afternoon when there are fewer visitors. Another option is to take a guided tour, which can provide insight into the history and significance of the site. Overall, Machu Picchu is a must-see destination for anyone visiting Peru or South America. The beauty, history, and cultural significance of the site are truly unmatched, and it's an experience that will stay with you for a lifetime. Machu Picchu es una antigua ciudadela inca ubicada en lo alto de las montañas de los Andes en Perú. El sitio es uno de los destinos más icónicos y impresionantemente hermosos del mundo, atrayendo a millones de visitantes cada año. Para llegar a Machu Picchu, los visitantes pueden hacer la famosa Camino Inca o tomar un tren desde la cercana ciudad de Cusco. Una vez allí, los visitantes pueden explorar las ruinas de la ciudadela, que incluyen impresionantes estructuras como templos, terrazas y plazas. La intrincada obra de piedra y los logros de ingeniería de los incas son realmente impresionantes. Aunque Machu Picchu puede llenarse de turistas, especialmente durante la temporada alta, hay formas de evitar las multitudes y aprovechar al máximo la experiencia. Una opción es visitar temprano en la mañana o más tarde por la tarde, cuando hay menos visitantes. Otra opción es tomar un recorrido guiado, que puede proporcionar información sobre la historia y la importancia del sitio. En general, Machu Picchu es un destino imperdible para cualquier persona que visite Perú o América del Sur. La belleza, la historia y la importancia cultural del sitio son realmente incomparables, y es una experiencia que permanecerá contigo para toda la vida.

text_clean: This is an amazing place! Incredible location in the exuberant ANDES . The tracks going to the point are unforgettable, that's the essence and uniqueness of those evergreen mountains. Rich full of life with wild rivers, dangerous trails and "pacha mama's " fruits of delight😂. The views and the natural phenomena situation make Machu Picchu a memorable place. So international!!! On the other hand stay the construction itself : very modest and elementary for the time ( Incas like all American tribes ) were underdeveloped and it is evident in all their monuments around the continent. Lack of any written documents make the stories sound so funny and absurd! Tour guides are so amateurish that make me LOL! Extremely overpriced but that's the sad story of the mass tourism industry all around the world now!

text_clean: The site itself is exactly what you see in the photos. The views around are nice. It's very crowded though, and buying tickets is a bureaucratic nightmare: You have to queue for a number that allows you to buy a ticket at a certain time. Then have to come back at that time, listen for your number (which is called out by some guy in Spanish to a crowd of hundreds), then queue again. Then you have to pick one of 6 routes and 3 additions, most of which will no longer be available in the upcoming days, depending on your luck. The routes that are available usually don't allow entrance to the actual site since those are booked way in advance via a terrible online system. If you want to go at the start of the year, you cannot book tickets in advance, because you can only by tickets for the current year. Also, this online system is down or unresponsive for most of January. Even though we booked through an agency 6 months in advance, they were not able to secure our tickets so we had to queue. In the end, our group were not able to actually visit the site, only take a photo from top.... There are so many nice things to see in Peru and South America... Including much larger and more impressive Inca settlements, which are much cheaper or even free to visit... It's really a shame the Peruvian makes it so difficult to visit this place. Unless you really really want to visit Machu Picchu, I would recommend spending your time elsewhere.
label: positivo

text_clean: Peru Rail and Inca Rail are the two train operators. I went with Inca Rail’s bimodal service based on trip times. I took The Voyager light option. Overall, it was a good experience. I took a bus from Cusco to Ollantaytambo, then a train to Aguas Calientes. I went the day before to avoid any potential delays. You can book everything as a package through either company, but I found it cheaper and more flexible to book tickets separately. You can either walk up to the entrance of Machu Picchu or take the bus ($12 USD one way). The buses are run by Consettur, and tickets can be bought online or at the ticket office. The ride takes about 25 minutes, and I recommend getting in line at least an hour before your entrance time. There are signs for different lines based on entry times. Make sure you have your ticket and passport before getting in line. While waiting, guides will offer their services. I was quoted $25 USD per person for a group of four or $100 USD for a private tour. I got in line around 7 am and reached the entrance around 8. Once you arrive, there’s a bathroom that costs 2 soles. There are no official bathrooms inside. There’s also a spot to get food and store luggage or a bag if needed. I visited at the end of January and was worried about the weather, so I booked two different routes on two separate days. I did the 2A classic route and 1A Machu Picchu Mountain. I had an 8 am entry time both days and thankfully had great weather. It seemed to get cloudier and rainier in the afternoon The 2A route was good. I went without a guide, but I heard others say they really enjoyed having one. It seemed like about half the visitors had guides, half didn’t. It took me about two hours to walk around, taking my time. The 2A classic route is popular for a reason, but I didn’t see any signs differentiating it from 2B, just a general sign for route 2. The highlight for me was hiking Machu Picchu Mountain. It wasn’t crowded at all. I saw maybe 10 people during the hike. The views were stunning, and while the hike is challenging, it’s absolutely worth it. You’re walking up stone steps for pretty much the entire way, so wearing shoes or hiking boots with good traction is a must. The trail is well-maintained. There are great viewpoints along the hike, so even if you can’t make it all the way up, you’ll still get good views. The first 75% is wide, then it gets narrower and steeper with some drop-offs, but nothing too intense. Make sure to bring water, bug spray, a raincoat, poncho, and sunscreen. Once you reach the top, you get a bird’s-eye view of the whole area, and it’s incredible. It took me about 1.5 hours to hike up and 1 hour to hike down.

input_modelo: aspecto: limpieza reseña: Peru Rail and Inca Rail are the two train operators. I went with Inca Rail’s bimodal service based on trip times. I took The Voyager light option. Overall, it was a good experience. I took a bus from Cusco to Ollantaytambo, then a train to Aguas Calientes. I went the day before to avoid any potential delays. You can book everything as a package through either company, but I found it cheaper and more flexible to book tickets separately. You can either walk up to the entrance of Machu Picchu or take the bus ($12 USD one way). The buses are run by Consettur, and tickets can be bought online or at the ticket office. The ride takes about 25 minutes, and I recommend getting in line at least an hour before your entrance time. There are signs for different lines based on entry times. Make sure you have your ticket and passport before getting in line. While waiting, guides will offer their services. I was quoted $25 USD per person for a group of four or $100 USD for a private tour. I got in line around 7 am and reached the entrance around 8. Once you arrive, there’s a bathroom that costs 2 soles. There are no official bathrooms inside. There’s also a spot to get food and store luggage or a bag if needed. I visited at the end of January and was worried about the weather, so I booked two different routes on two separate days. I did the 2A classic route and 1A Machu Picchu Mountain. I had an 8 am entry time both days and thankfully had great weather. It seemed to get cloudier and rainier in the afternoon The 2A route was good. I went without a guide, but I heard others say they really enjoyed having one. It seemed like about half the visitors had guides, half didn’t. It took me about two hours to walk around, taking my time. The 2A classic route is popular for a reason, but I didn’t see any signs differentiating it from 2B, just a general sign for route 2. The highlight for me was hiking Machu Picchu Mountain. It wasn’t crowded at all. I saw maybe 10 people during the hike. The views were stunning, and while the hike is challenging, it’s absolutely worth it. You’re walking up stone steps for pretty much the entire way, so wearing shoes or hiking boots with good traction is a must. The trail is well-maintained. There are great viewpoints along the hike, so even if you can’t make it all the way up, you’ll still get good views. The first 75% is wide, then it gets narrower and steeper with some drop-offs, but nothing too intense. Make sure to bring water, bug spray, a raincoat, poncho, and sunscreen. Once you reach the top, you get a bird’s-eye view of the whole area, and it’s incredible. It took me about 1.5 hours to hike up and 1 hour to hike down.

text_clean: Pretty dope! For all those unfamiliar there are only 2 ways to get to the small town at the base of MP, by train ($150+ USD round trip from cusco) or by foot (very expensive multi day trip). It costs about $150 round trip to fly to Cusco. Entrance to MP is around $40+ USD. From Aguas calientes (small town as the base of MP) you can hike up or take a bus ($24 USD each way). Recommend getting the wynapicchu ticket ($65-70 USD with entry), it lets you reenter after your hike. Pretty good option if you do the 10am hike, you can go through with your guide, hike, exit to eat and hydrate and then reenter to take your time. Private guides cost about $70 USD for a private 2-3hour tour. Make sure they have their ID badge. Guides have to be certified by the government, so check them so you don’t get skunked! Group tours are up to 14 people and range $10-20 USD per person. Guides take local and foreign currency. Make sure to speak with them a bit in depth to make sure they speak your language. I’ve heard of bilingual tours where one language group recurved much more information than the other. There is no food allowed inside MP, and there are no bathrooms in MP, you have to exit to use to toilet and standard tickets don’t allow reentry. You also cannot backtrack, there are staff that watch to make sure people move through. There are certain sections such as the temple of the condor that are only open for a limited amount of time. Recommend hitting the sun temple in the am, hike and then seeing the condor temple on the way out as this closes at 1pm. You can also hike the Inca ridge once inside for free (40 minute round trip) or the sun gate for free (2 hour round trip). Some people like to get in early to watch the sunrise through the sun gate. There are llamas all over the site, they are really friendly. Bring LOTS of water, especially if you’re hiking. They sell small water bottles for about $2.50. Sometimes you can use card for water and snacks. It depends whose working at the ice cream stand. They woman working clearly didn’t like tourists and told us cash only. We walked past 10 minutes later and she was using a card for a local... lol. Pretty magical place! Make sure to do some strength training and cardio beforehand. Eat a huge breakfast before you get on the bus. Oh and just be prepared to wait in line for your bus with literally 1000 other people going up at the same time. I find that knowing tips ahead of time helps with the anxiety of a strange place.
label: neutro

text_clean: Unbelievable in person. Pictures do not do it justice. Here’s some helpful tips as an American tourist on their first trip to Macchu Picchu: 1 Buy your tickets well in advance. There is a huge line at the tourist office in Aguas Calientes for last minute ticket purchases. It’s chaotic, skip the headache and plan ahead. We were out in Aguas Calientes for two nights and both nights the line / crowd was huge. Do your research on the best time to go. We went early October (last week) - the temperature was perfect. Crispy in the early morning then warmed up later on. Sun is strong, wear sunscreen and bug spray. 2 If you can, and are up for the challenge, do the hiking trail up. Not the Inca Trail. There’s a walking path that’s about ~1+ hour (longer if you take breaks). It’s 100% worth the effort for the views. It’s like the stair-master on hard. There are buses that run very frequently up but they’re not for the faint of heart or for impatient people who hate waiting in long lines. The buses fly up the sides of the mountain (no thank you). 3 Get an early ticket. It gets crowded. We had a 9am and it was great. It was crowded when we were leaving about 2 hours later. 4 There’s a snack counter and a formal restaurant. The snack counter is 10/10 with beer, cocktails, desserts, and snacks (chicken empanada is A1). Grab something before you go in as you can’t eat when you’re in there. 5 You cannot get in ahead of your ticket time. They’re strict about this. 6 BRING YOUR PASSPORT 7 Get a guide. There’s no informational signs that describe what you’re looking at. If you don’t have someone describing things you’re just there for the views (even though we left with more questions than answers) 8 Bring soles for the bathroom. 2 soles to enter. 9 Respect the beauty of the site
label: neutro

text_clean: Pristine from start to finish. The train is a clean and comfortable 3.5-4 hour ride from Cusco. Following the train ride, you will enter Machu Pichu City where you will take a short bus ride to the historical site. Please beware, you will still have a fairly strenuous walk/hike from the bus to the site. If you live in a flat area, I suggest using a stair climber to prepare yourself. My tour, which I booked through AirBnB experiences was a one on one, personalized experience. This allowed me to rest when needed, ask questions, and make correlations. Most of the groups were 20+ people, I don't suggest that. You want to observe and absorb, so book an intimate tour. Also, important to note, there are 3 tour levels. Level one only grants access to having your picture taken close to the site, without touring the site. Level 2 grants the most access overall, but it does not focus on the royalty sites like Level 3. There is also an option to add a hike to Winchu Pichu (the mountain in the background of the pictures). That option is only available twice per day. I would never, so I'm not sure how to book it 😆🤣😆🤣 PLEASE PLEASE PLEASE bring only minimal gear, otherwise you will be rucking. A sweat suit with poncho or a lightweight all weather jacket will suffice. Layer the clothing so you can add/subtract as needed. Apply bug spray before leaving your hotel. Carry only one bottle of water. Fanny pack + water is all you need. You can buy snacks before/after in the city. YOU MUST HAVE YOUR PASSPORT TO ENTER/ EXIT THE SITE. P.S. entry is limited to a few thousand people per day, please book well in advance to secure your spot. Also, the rainy season starts in October. I was very blessed to only endure a sprinkle. With a hard rain, you won't be able to see and it will be very dangerous. You are walking/hiking on stones the entire time.
label: neutro

text_clean: La entrada cuesta 4 soles, cuándo fui sólo funcionaba el primer parque que está en Petit Thouars, no funcionaba el de la Av Arequipa porque decían estaba en mantenimiento, el parque está muy bonito y el espectáculo de luces empieza 6:30 pm al parecer, al entrar me pareció extraño ver tantas señoras con muchas bolsas pensé venían de viaje de la sierra, pero casi a las 6 pm empezaron a sacar su mercadería de las bolsas para vender dentro del parque, así perjudican a las personas que alquilan dentro del parque un local y venden, desde la boletería y el personal del parque se hacen de la vista gorda y dejan pasar ambulantes y eso le quita el orden, los churros dentro del parque cuestan 3 soles y los que venden los ambulantes dentro cuesta 1 sol y es pasado y feo. Claro que el choripán que venden dentro del parque cuesta 5 soles y es horrible no lo compren, pero hay otros negocios y los ambulantes perjudican esos negocios.Lo demás el circuito de las aguas y limpieza están muy bien. Le pregunté a los vigilantes por qué dejan entrar ambulantes y no sabían que responderme.
label: neutro

text_clean: Stunning views of the backside of Machu Picchu where few visitors venture. This trail leads to one of the original entrances to Machu Picchu, a short foot bridge built into the side of a cliff. The trial leads to a gate where you can see the footbridge from a distance. This trail is not very difficult physically, but keep in mind there are sheer cliffs along the way with no railing at all. If you are afraid of heights or have children, you need to be very careful as one slip could be tragic. Save the selfies for another place, it’s too dangerous to take chances here on this trail.
